summary: A brief overview of René Descartes' conclusions in his second Meditation, specifically his realization that he must exist if he is being deceived by an evil demon, and his subsequent definition of himself as a 'thinking thing' engaged in various mental activities.

principles.statement: I must exist if I am being deceived.
principles.rationale: The very act of being deceived by a hypothetical evil demon necessitates a subject who is experiencing the deception.
principles.source_quote: even if there is an evil demon who turns all of his efforts to deceiving Descartes, he must exist if he is being deceived.

principles.statement: I am fundamentally a thinking thing.
principles.rationale: The self is defined by its capacity for a number of different kinds of thinking and distinct types of mental activities.

mental_models.name: The Evil Demon
mental_models.description: A hypothetical scenario where a malicious entity attempts to deceive the thinker about everything, used as a radical skeptical lens to find an indubitable truth.
mental_models.example: Descartes uses this model to determine that even if an evil demon turns all efforts to deceiving him, he must still exist in order to be deceived.

signature_quotes.context: Answering the question 'what am I?' after establishing his own existence.
